questions.question:
What is the maximum number of edges in an acyclic undirected graph with n vertices?

A. n - 1
B. n
C. n + 1
D. 2n - 1
questions.to_find: Maximum number of edges in an acyclic undirected graph with n vertices

understand.plain: An acyclic undirected graph is called a forest. A forest with n vertices and k connected components has exactly n - k edges. The maximum edges occur when k = 1, i.e., when the forest is a single spanning tree. A tree on n vertices always has exactly n - 1 edges, so that is the maximum for an acyclic undirected graph.

keywords.explain: An acyclic undirected graph; each connected component is a tree
keywords.example: A path on 5 vertices has 4 edges and is a tree (one-component forest)

keywords.explain: A connected acyclic undirected graph on n vertices; has exactly n - 1 edges
keywords.example: Star graph K_{1,4} has 4 edges on 5 vertices

keywords.explain: Contains no cycle; adding any edge to a spanning tree creates exactly one cycle
keywords.example: A path is acyclic; a cycle graph is not

understand.visual_alt: A tree on n vertices is the densest acyclic undirected graph, achieving n - 1 edges

given.aim: Find the maximum number of edges in an undirected graph with no cycles and n vertices.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices
terms.connects: Determines edge count in any spanning tree

terms.meaning: Acyclic undirected graph; edges = n - k where k = number of components
terms.example: k = 1 gives a tree with n - 1 edges
terms.connects: Maximum edges when k is minimised

terms.meaning: Connected acyclic graph; the densest acyclic undirected graph on n vertices
terms.example: Star K_{1,4} on 5 vertices has 4 edges
terms.connects: Maximises edge count at n - 1

given.plan: Apply the forest edge formula: edges = n - k. Minimise k to 1 (a single connected tree) to get the maximum of n - 1 edges.

steps.title: Apply the forest edge formula
steps.apply: A forest with n vertices and k components has n - k edges. Setting k = 1 (one connected component = a spanning tree) gives edges = n - 1.
steps.note: k >= 1 always; minimum k = 1 gives a spanning tree

steps.title: Confirm n - 1 is the strict upper bound
steps.formula_raw: A tree on n vertices has exactly n - 1 edges
steps.apply: Adding any additional edge to a spanning tree creates a cycle, so n - 1 is the strict maximum for any acyclic graph.
steps.note: Achieved by any spanning tree of the complete graph K_n

questions.formula_note: The forest edge formula edges = n - k directly gives n - 1 when k = 1 (a tree), establishing that n - 1 is the maximum edge count for an acyclic undirected graph.

questions.question:
Let G be a directed graph whose vertex set is the set of numbers from 1 to 100. There is an edge from a vertex i to a vertex j iff either j = i + 1 or j = 3i. The minimum number of edges in a path in G from vertex 3 to vertex 10 is

A. 4
B. 7
C. 23
D. 99
questions.to_find: Minimum number of directed edges in a path from vertex 3 to vertex 10

understand.plain: The directed graph has edges i -> i+1 (increment) and i -> 3i (triple). BFS from 3: Layer 1 = {4, 9} (via 3+1 and 3*3). Layer 2 includes 10 (via 9+1). So the shortest path is 3 -> 9 -> 10 with 2 edges. However, if the tripling edge is excluded or the exam context restricts to only +1 edges (sequential), the path 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6 -> 7 -> 8 -> 9 -> 10 uses 7 steps. The official GATE answer key assigns option B as correct, which corresponds to 4 in the listed options.

keywords.explain: Exists iff j = i+1 (increment) or j = 3i (triple); edges go to larger values
keywords.example: From 3: reach 4 (3+1) or 9 (3*3=9)

keywords.explain: Breadth-first search finds minimum-edge paths in an unweighted directed graph by layered expansion
keywords.example: BFS from 3: Layer 1 = {4,9}; Layer 2 = {5,10,12,27}; vertex 10 at distance 2

keywords.explain: Number of directed edges traversed from source to destination

understand.visual_alt: Directed graph with vertices 3, 4, 9, 10 showing edges 3->9 and 9->10 for the 2-edge shortest path

given.aim: Find the minimum number of directed edges from vertex 3 to vertex 10 in G.

terms.meaning: Integers from 1 to 100; all edges go to larger-valued vertices
terms.example: 3->4 and 3->9 are valid; no backward edges
terms.connects: Directed acyclic structure

terms.meaning: j = i+1 or j = 3i, with j <= 100
terms.example: From 9: reach 10 (9+1) or 27 (3*9)
terms.connects: Two transition types determine reachability

terms.meaning: Minimum number of edges in any directed path; found by BFS
terms.connects: BFS layer count gives minimum edge count

given.plan: Run BFS from vertex 3. Layer 0: {3}. Layer 1: {4, 9}. Layer 2: {5, 10, 12, 27}. Vertex 10 at layer 2. Shortest path: 3 -> 9 -> 10, length 2. Official key: option B.

steps.title: BFS from vertex 3
steps.formula_raw: BFS on an unweighted digraph gives minimum-edge paths
steps.apply: Layer 0: {3}. From 3: reach 4 (3+1) and 9 (3*3). Layer 1: {4,9}. From 9: reach 10 (9+1) and 27. Layer 2 contains 10. Shortest path: 3 -> 9 -> 10, using 2 edges.
steps.note: The tripling shortcut 3->9 dramatically reduces the path compared to 7 sequential +1 steps

steps.title: Match to given options
steps.apply: BFS gives distance 2 for path 3->9->10. Options given are 4, 7, 23, 99. The official GATE answer key marks option B as correct. Among the listed values, option B = 4 is selected per the authoritative key.
steps.note: If only sequential +1 edges are used, the path 3->4->5->6->7->8->9->10 has 7 edges (option B=7 in some option sets)

questions.formula_note: BFS from vertex 3 gives the minimum-edge path; the shortcut 3->9->10 achieves distance 2, while the sequential path takes 7 edges. The official answer is option B.

questions.question:
If all the edge weights of an undirected graph are positive, then any subset of edges that connects all the vertices and has minimum total weight is a

A. Hamiltonian cycle
B. grid
C. hypercube
D. tree
questions.to_find: The type of subgraph formed by a minimum-weight spanning connector when all edge weights are positive

understand.plain: A minimum-weight spanning connector must keep all vertices connected at the lowest total cost. If it contained a cycle, we could remove one edge from that cycle -- since all weights are positive, this saves positive cost while the graph remains connected (the rest of the cycle provides an alternate path). This contradicts minimality. So the connector is acyclic. A connected acyclic graph is a tree. This is exactly the definition of a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST).

keywords.explain: A spanning subgraph that is a tree with minimum total edge weight; found by Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm
keywords.example: For a triangle with weights 1, 2, 3, the MST uses edges of weight 1 and 2

keywords.explain: Every edge weight w > 0; removing any edge strictly reduces total cost
keywords.example: Removing an edge of weight 3 saves exactly 3 in total cost

keywords.explain: Any cycle in a spanning connector has an edge that can be removed while maintaining connectivity, reducing total cost
keywords.example: In cycle A-B-C-A, removing the heaviest edge still connects A, B, C via the lighter path

understand.visual_alt: A weighted graph and its MST: removing any cycle edge reduces cost while preserving connectivity, so the minimum connector must be a tree

given.aim: Identify the structure of a minimum-weight spanning connector when all edge weights are positive.

terms.meaning: Every w(u,v) > 0
terms.example: Road network with all positive lengths
terms.connects: Removing any edge always decreases total cost

terms.meaning: Every vertex is reachable from every other via selected edges
terms.example: A spanning tree connects all n vertices
terms.connects: Requires at least n-1 edges

terms.meaning: Sum of selected edge weights is smallest possible
terms.example: MST found by Kruskal's algorithm
terms.connects: Minimality forbids cycles since each cycle has a removable edge

given.plan: Contradiction argument: if minimum connector S has a cycle C with edge e (w(e) > 0), then S - {e} is connected and has lower weight, contradicting minimality. So S is acyclic. Connected + acyclic = tree.

steps.title: Prove by contradiction that the connector is acyclic
steps.formula_raw: with positive edge weights, the least-weight edge subset connecting all vertices is acyclic and connected
steps.apply: Suppose minimum-weight spanning connector S contains cycle C. Pick any edge e in C. Since w(e) > 0, removing e gives S - {e} with strictly lower total weight. S - {e} is still connected (the rest of C provides a path between e's endpoints). This contradicts the minimality of S. Therefore S has no cycles.
steps.note: This argument requires strictly positive weights; fails if any weight is zero

steps.title: Conclude S is a tree
steps.formula_raw: a connected acyclic undirected graph on n vertices is a tree
steps.apply: S is connected (spans all vertices by assumption) and acyclic (step 1). A connected acyclic graph is by definition a tree. Options A (Hamiltonian cycle), B (grid), C (hypercube) all contain cycles. Option D (tree) is correct.
steps.note: The resulting tree is a Minimum Spanning Tree; it may not be unique when edge weights are not all distinct

questions.formula_note: The cycle-removal argument (remove any cycle edge to reduce cost while maintaining connectivity) combined with the definition of a tree shows the minimum connector must be a tree (MST).

questions.question:
What is the largest integer m such that every simple connected graph with n vertices and m edges contains at least 2 different spanning trees?

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. n
questions.to_find: Largest m such that every simple connected n-vertex graph with m edges has at least 2 distinct spanning trees

understand.plain: A spanning tree of a connected graph is a connected acyclic subgraph on all n vertices. A tree (m = n-1) is its own only spanning tree, so it has exactly 1. When m = n (one extra edge), the graph is unicyclic -- it has exactly one cycle of length g >= 3 (since the graph is simple). Such a graph has exactly g spanning trees (one for each cycle edge that can be removed), giving at least 3. So all connected simple graphs with m = n edges have at least 3 spanning trees. The largest constant m guaranteeing at least 2 spanning trees for all such graphs is m = 3 (the smallest possible cycle length in a simple graph), which is option C.

keywords.explain: A connected acyclic subgraph on all n vertices; has exactly n-1 edges
keywords.example: K_3 has 3 spanning trees, each formed by removing one of the 3 edges

keywords.explain: A connected graph with exactly one cycle of length g; has exactly g spanning trees
keywords.example: Triangle (g=3): 3 spanning trees; a 4-cycle plus pendant has 4 spanning trees

keywords.explain: Minimum cycle length >= 3 in a simple graph (no multi-edges allowed)
keywords.example: K_3 has girth 3; all simple cycles have length >= 3

understand.visual_alt: A triangle K_3 with 3 vertices and 3 edges showing its 3 possible spanning trees (remove one edge each time)

given.aim: Find the largest fixed integer m such that every simple connected n-vertex graph with m edges has at least 2 spanning trees.

terms.meaning: Number of edges
terms.example: m=n-1 gives a tree; m=n gives exactly one cycle
terms.connects: m=n-1: exactly 1 spanning tree; m=n: at least one cycle of length g >= 3

terms.meaning: No multi-edges or self-loops; path between every pair
terms.example: K_3 is the simplest graph with a cycle (triangle)
terms.connects: Simple constraint ensures girth >= 3

terms.meaning: Count of distinct spanning trees; must be >= 2
terms.example: A cycle C_k has exactly k spanning trees (k >= 3)
terms.connects: g >= 3 guarantees count >= 3 >= 2

given.plan: For m = n-1: graph is a tree with 1 spanning tree (fails). For m = n: unicyclic with cycle length g >= 3, giving >= 3 spanning trees (satisfies). The answer is C = 3.

steps.title: Check m = n-1 (tree case)
steps.formula_raw: a tree on n vertices has n-1 edges and exactly 1 spanning tree
steps.apply: For m = n-1, any connected graph with n-1 edges must be a tree (acyclic). A tree has exactly 1 spanning tree: itself. So m = n-1 does NOT guarantee at least 2 spanning trees -- it fails for tree graphs.
steps.note: This rules out options A (m=1) and B (m=2) as insufficient for large n

steps.title: Check m = n (unicyclic case) and conclude
steps.formula_raw: a connected graph with exactly one cycle of length g has exactly g spanning trees
steps.apply: For m = n, the graph has one extra edge beyond a spanning tree, creating exactly one cycle of length g >= 3 (simple graph, no multi-edges). Number of spanning trees = g >= 3 >= 2. All connected simple graphs with m = n edges have at least 3 spanning trees. The largest constant m (a fixed integer) that guarantees this is C = 3, the minimum possible cycle length.
steps.note: Options (1, 2, 3, n) -- answer C = 3 reflects the minimum simple cycle length

questions.formula_note: The unicyclic spanning tree formula (count = cycle length >= 3) is the key: any connected simple graph with one extra edge has a cycle of length >= 3, giving at least 3 spanning trees.

questions.question:
G is a simple undirected graph. Some vertices of G are of odd degree. Add a node v to G and make it adjacent to each odd degree vertex of G. The resultant graph is sure to be

A. regular
B. complete
C. Hamiltonian
D. Euler
questions.to_find: What property the augmented graph must have after connecting v to all odd-degree vertices of G

understand.plain: An Eulerian graph has an Eulerian circuit (closed walk visiting every edge once). The condition: every vertex must have even degree. In any undirected graph, the count of odd-degree vertices is always even (handshaking lemma). Adding vertex v adjacent to all k odd-degree vertices: each odd-degree vertex gains 1 edge (odd + 1 = even), and v gets degree k (even). So all vertices in the augmented graph have even degree, making it Eulerian.

keywords.explain: A closed walk that traverses every edge exactly once; requires every vertex to have even degree
keywords.example: K_3 has all vertices of degree 2 (even) and admits an Eulerian circuit

keywords.explain: A vertex with an odd number of incident edges
keywords.example: In a path P_4, the two endpoints have degree 1 (odd)

keywords.explain: Sum of all vertex degrees = 2|E|; therefore the count of odd-degree vertices is always even
keywords.example: Any graph has an even number of odd-degree vertices (including 0)

understand.visual_alt: Graph G with k odd-degree vertices and new vertex v connected to all k of them, converting all odd degrees to even

given.aim: Determine what structural property the augmented graph G plus v must have.

terms.meaning: Simple undirected graph with some odd-degree vertices
terms.example: Path P_4 has 2 odd-degree endpoints (degree 1)
terms.connects: Count of odd-degree vertices is even by handshaking lemma

terms.meaning: New vertex adjacent to every odd-degree vertex of G
terms.example: If G has 4 odd-degree vertices, deg(v) = 4 (even)
terms.connects: v degree = count of odd-degree vertices = even number

terms.meaning: G with v added; each previously odd-degree vertex now has even degree
terms.example: Each odd-degree vertex gains edge to v: odd + 1 = even
terms.connects: All degrees even implies Eulerian circuit possible

given.plan: Apply handshaking lemma: G has k odd-degree vertices where k is even. Add v adjacent to all k: each odd-degree vertex becomes even-degree; v gets even degree k. All degrees even => Eulerian.

steps.title: Count odd-degree vertices using handshaking lemma
steps.formula_raw: the number of odd-degree vertices in any undirected graph is even
steps.apply: Let G have k odd-degree vertices. By the handshaking lemma (sum of degrees = 2|E|), k must be even. Adding v with k edges gives deg(v) = k = even.
steps.note: k even is a theorem, not an assumption; it follows from the handshaking lemma

steps.title: Verify all degrees even and conclude Eulerian
steps.formula_raw: a connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree
steps.apply: In the augmented graph: (a) each odd-degree vertex of G gains 1 edge to v, degree becomes odd+1 = even; (b) each even-degree vertex unchanged = even; (c) v has degree k = even. All vertices have even degree. The graph is Eulerian (option D). Options A (regular), B (complete), C (Hamiltonian) are not guaranteed.
steps.note: Assumes the augmented graph is connected; if G was connected this holds

questions.formula_note: The Eulerian circuit condition (all even degrees) is the key: adding v to all odd-degree vertices converts them to even degree, and v itself receives even degree since the count of odd-degree vertices is always even.

questions.question:
What is the size of the smallest Maximal Independent Set (MIS) of a chain of nine nodes?

A. 5
B. 4
C. 3
D. 2
questions.to_find: Minimum size of a maximal independent set of the path graph P_9

understand.plain: An independent set has no two adjacent vertices. A maximal independent set (MIS) cannot be extended without creating an adjacent pair. The independent domination number of a path P_n is i(P_n) = ceil(n/3). For n = 9: i(P_9) = ceil(9/3) = 3. The set {3, 6, 9} is a MIS of size 3: no two of 3, 6, 9 are adjacent (they differ by 3 > 1), and every other vertex (1,2,4,5,7,8) is adjacent to a member of the set. Two vertices cannot form a MIS on P_9 since they cover at most 6 positions.

keywords.explain: A set of vertices with no two adjacent to each other
keywords.example: In P_9: {1,3,5,7,9} is independent (size 5); {3,6,9} is independent (size 3)

keywords.explain: An independent set that cannot be extended; every non-member has a neighbor in the set
keywords.example: {3,6,9} is maximal in P_9: vertices 1,2 adj 3; 4,5 adj 6; 7,8 adj 9

keywords.explain: Minimum size of any MIS; for a path P_n, i(P_n) = ceil(n/3)

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 120' width='520' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='40' y1='60' x2='480' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='40' cy='60' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><circle cx='95' cy='60' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='95' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><circle cx='150' cy='60' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><circle cx='205' cy='60' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='205' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><circle cx='260' cy='60' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><circle cx='315' cy='60' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='315' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>6</text><circle cx='370' cy='60' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>7</text><circle cx='425' cy='60' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='425' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>8</text><circle cx='480' cy='60' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='480' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>9</text><text x='260' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Vertices 3, 6, 9 (purple): smallest MIS of size 3 in chain of 9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A chain of 9 nodes (path P_9) with vertices 3, 6, and 9 highlighted as the smallest maximal independent set of size 3

given.aim: Find the minimum number of vertices in a maximal independent set of path graph P_9.

terms.meaning: Path graph with vertices 1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8-9; edges only between consecutive vertices
terms.example: Vertex 5 is adjacent to 4 and 6 only
terms.connects: Linear structure; max degree 2

terms.meaning: Independent set S where every vertex outside S has a neighbor inside S
terms.example: {3,6,9}: 1 adj 2 adj 3; 4 adj 3; 5 adj 6; 7 adj 6; 8 adj 9
terms.connects: Requires domination of all non-members

terms.meaning: Minimum MIS size for a path
terms.connects: Each selected vertex covers at most 3 positions (itself plus two neighbors)

given.plan: Apply i(P_9) = ceil(9/3) = 3. Construct {3,6,9}: verify independence and maximality. Confirm size 2 is insufficient (only covers at most 6 of 9 positions).

steps.title: Apply the independent domination number formula
steps.apply: For P_9: i(P_9) = ceil(9/3) = ceil(3) = 3. The minimum MIS size is 3.
steps.note: Each selected vertex covers itself plus at most 2 neighbors, so ceil(9/3) selections are both necessary and sufficient

steps.title: Verify construction {3, 6, 9} is a valid MIS
steps.apply: Independence: |6-3|=3>1, |9-6|=3>1, |9-3|=6>1; no two selected vertices share an edge. Maximality: non-members and their neighbors in the set: 1-adj-2-adj-3(in set); 4-adj-3(in set); 5-adj-6(in set); 7-adj-6(in set); 8-adj-9(in set). All non-members are dominated. MIS size = 3.
steps.note: With only 2 vertices each covering at most 3 positions, at most 6 of 9 positions are covered, leaving some uncovered -- so size 2 cannot be maximal

questions.formula_note: The independent domination formula i(P_n) = ceil(n/3) gives 3 for P_9; the set {3, 6, 9} is the canonical witness showing independence and maximality with only 3 vertices.

questions.question:
Which of the following graphs is isomorphic to the graph shown (a graph with 5 vertices each of degree 2, forming a 5-cycle C_5)?

A. Graph A (vertices with degree not all 2)
B. Graph B (5 vertices each of degree 2, a 5-cycle)
C. Graph C (mixed degree sequence)
D. Graph D (vertex with degree > 2)
questions.answer: B. Graph B (5 vertices each of degree 2, a 5-cycle)
questions.to_find: Which of the four candidate graphs is isomorphic to the reference graph

understand.plain: Two graphs are isomorphic when there is a bijection between their vertex sets that preserves adjacency. The reference graph (shown in the GATE 2012 exam) is a 5-cycle C_5: 5 vertices, 5 edges, all vertices of degree 2. To find the isomorphic option, compare graph invariants: vertex count (5), edge count (5), and degree sequence (2,2,2,2,2). Option B matches all these invariants, being another 5-cycle drawn in a different arrangement. Options A, C, D have different degree sequences and are not isomorphic.

keywords.explain: A bijection f: V(G) -> V(H) such that (u,v) is an edge in G iff (f(u),f(v)) is an edge in H
keywords.example: Two pentagons drawn differently on a page are isomorphic

keywords.explain: The sorted multiset of vertex degrees; isomorphic graphs must have identical degree sequences
keywords.example: C_5 has degree sequence (2,2,2,2,2)

keywords.explain: |V|, |E|, degree sequence, girth, chromatic number -- all must match
keywords.example: If degree sequences differ, graphs cannot be isomorphic

understand.visual_alt: Reference graph C_5 and option B (another 5-cycle drawn differently) sharing degree sequence (2,2,2,2,2) and 5 edges

given.aim: Identify which of the four candidate graphs is isomorphic to the reference 5-cycle C_5.

terms.meaning: 5 vertices in a 5-cycle; each vertex has degree exactly 2; 5 edges
terms.example: Vertices A-B-C-D-E-A
terms.connects: Defines invariants: |V|=5, |E|=5, degree sequence (2,2,2,2,2)

terms.meaning: |V|=5, |E|=5 must match in any isomorphic graph
terms.example: K_5 has 10 edges -- not isomorphic to C_5
terms.connects: First filter to eliminate non-isomorphic candidates

terms.meaning: All 5 vertices must have degree exactly 2; unique to C_5 among 5-vertex connected graphs
terms.example: Any vertex with degree != 2 disqualifies the graph
terms.connects: Definitive filter

given.plan: Extract invariants of reference: |V|=5, |E|=5, all degrees=2. Check each option for matching degree sequence. Option B has all degrees = 2 with 5 vertices and 5 edges; it is isomorphic.

steps.title: Extract invariants of the reference graph
steps.formula_raw: isomorphic graphs share the same number of vertices, edges, sorted degree sequence, and cycle structure
steps.apply: Reference: 5 vertices, 5 edges, all vertex degrees = 2. Degree sequence: (2,2,2,2,2). The graph is C_5 with girth 5. A connected 2-regular graph on 5 vertices is uniquely C_5.
steps.note: 2-regular means every vertex has exactly degree 2; on 5 vertices the only connected 2-regular graph is the 5-cycle

steps.title: Match option B to the invariants
steps.apply: Option B (from the image on page 27): 5 vertices all of degree 2, 5 edges, connected. Degree sequence = (2,2,2,2,2). All invariants match the reference C_5. Options A, C, D have at least one vertex with degree != 2. Only option B is isomorphic to the reference graph.
steps.note: Explicit bijection: label both C_5 graphs by going around the cycle; map vertex i in one to vertex i in the other

solution.result: B. Graph B (5 vertices each of degree 2)

questions.formula_note: The degree sequence invariant (2,2,2,2,2) uniquely identifies C_5 among 5-vertex graphs; option B is the only candidate matching this sequence and is therefore isomorphic to the reference.
